entries.title: John Tabor
entries.body:
**Role:** City Councilor, co-chair Housing Committee and Blue Ribbon Housing Committee

**Background:** Retired publisher, Seacoast Media Group (grew from $4.5M to $29M). Yale grad. Elected to council after retiring in 2018.

**Committees:** Legislative Affairs, Audit, Housing, Governance, Energy Advisory, Fee

**Housing record:**
- Led Sherburne School project to 127 affordable units
- Championed the GNOD
- Co-chaired Portsmouth Listens for 20 years
- Pushed 50/50 market rate / affordable model
- Initiated Flashvote electronic polling for community engagement

**Philosophy:** Community process is non-negotiable. Data matters. Incremental wins compound. Government can be a force for good.

**Key quote:** 'We need 1,500 units in the next two years to balance our market.'

**Contact:** Via city website form (public record)

entries.title: Craig Welch
entries.body:
**Role:** Executive Director, Portsmouth Housing Authority

**Contact:** craigwelch@nh-pha.com / 603-436-4310 x118

**Notes:** Operational lead for the PHA. Has been the implementation partner for Ruth Griffin Place and Lafayette Road projects.

entries.title: Joanna Kelley
entries.body:
**Role:** Assistant Mayor, co-chair Housing Blue Ribbon Committee with Tabor

**Focus:** Project sizing and community buy-in. Learned from Pannaway/Sherburne backlash — 'everything in terms of development is balance, balance in terms of aesthetics and balance of size.'

**Notes:** More cautious than Tabor on density numbers. Important to understand her position when proposals come to committee.

entries.title: Peter Stith
entries.body:
**Role:** City Planning Manager

**Function:** Manages zoning ordinance changes, supports Planning Board process. Key staff contact for the 2027 zoning rewrite.

**Notes:** Will be the person shepherding SB 284 and HB 577 compliance amendments through the Planning Board.

entries.title: Deaglan McEachern
entries.body:
**Role:** Mayor of Portsmouth, currently serving third term. Elected 2021 by receiving highest vote count among 9 councilors — Portsmouth's system makes the top vote-getter mayor.

**Background:** Raised in Portsmouth, lives in the Islington Creek neighborhood across from the house his grandfather built in 1940. Deep family roots in Portsmouth politics — father Paul McEachern was City Councilor and Assistant Mayor; aunt Mary McEachern Keenan was Mayor 1986-87. Works in tech sector (Smarsh). Masters in History from Cambridge University; BA from UC Berkeley. Former US national rowing team member — represented US at World Championship level, won the Oxford-Cambridge Boat Race in 2010.

**Housing record:** Funded the Housing Trust, strengthened zoning for affordable units, partnered with PHA to create mixed-income housing. Co-created Citizen Response Task Force during COVID to support local businesses.

**Political trajectory:** Seriously exploring a run for NH Governor in 2026. Has met with Democratic Governors Association officials. Strong on housing and affordability as a statewide issue — calls it his signature issue.

**Relevance:** Connected with him in context of Housing Authority board application (March 2026). Key relationship to maintain — if he runs for governor, housing policy in Portsmouth becomes even more of a proving ground.

**Contact:** Mayor's Office 603-610-7200

entries.title: Beth Moreau
entries.body:
**Role:** City Councilor. Member of Housing Blue Ribbon Committee. Leads the Zoning Working Group within the committee.

**Background:** Residential real estate attorney (16 years). BS from Southern NH University, JD from New England School of Law. Served on Portsmouth Planning Board 2013-2021 — Vice Chair for 7 years. Also serves as Vice Chair of Rockingham Planning Commission. Former board president of Haven (domestic violence org).

**Housing record:**
- Worked on ADU ordinance updates to remove barriers
- Updated downtown incentives to require workforce housing in larger buildings
- Helped secure two InvestNH grants including $250k for a Community Housing Navigator
- Championing payments-in-lieu system for workforce housing feeding into Housing Trust
- Supported the GNOD unanimously

**Zoning Working Group:** Leading the committee's deep dive into what legal constraints exist, what other NH cities (Nashua) have done with enabling legislation, and how Portsmouth can reform its ordinance ahead of the 2027 rewrite.

**Key quote:** "Stop talking about it and start doing it."

**Nuance:** More cautious on pace than Tabor — has noted "a lot of pushback that we're trying to rush this." Comes at housing from a legal/process angle vs. Tabor's political/community engagement angle. Has planning board institutional knowledge that nobody else on the council has.

entries.title: Annie Hopkins
entries.body:
**Role:** City Councilor, first elected November 2025 (first-time candidate). Sworn in January 2026 for current term alongside the full re-elected council.

**Background:** Mother of a toddler (and infant). Comes at city issues from a young-family, lived-experience perspective. Part of the 603 Forward-aligned slate. Impressed observers at Greater Portsmouth Chamber Collaborative roundtables at Strawbery Banke during her campaign.

**Focus areas:** Childcare affordability and workforce participation. Currently leading council investigation into whether Portsmouth can convene a city-level childcare task force. Key data point she cites: median NH family income ~$112k, but toddler + infant care averages ~$32k/year (28% of income vs. the federal 7% affordability guideline).

**Housing relevance:** Frames childcare and housing as linked workforce issues — families can't stay in Portsmouth if they can't afford either. The Lafayette Road / Christ Church project (PHA + HAVEN + childcare) directly addresses this intersection.

**Notes:** Newer voice on the council, less institutional history than Moreau or Tabor. Worth watching as someone who represents the young-family constituency that housing and childcare policy is meant to serve. Fresh mandate from 2025 election.

entries.title: Kate Cook
entries.body:
**Role:** City Councilor, currently serving multiple terms. Reelected 2025.

**Background:** Former U.S. diplomat at the U.S. Mission to the United Nations. BA from Duke University (Political Science and French). Past board chair for Southern District YMCA. Lives in Portsmouth's South End (FOSE board member). Established the Council's Governance Committee, overhauling ethics processes.

**Housing record:** One of the most vocal and consistent housing voices on the council. In 2023 introduced the motion that eventually led to the GNOD concept. Championed the overlay model publicly during the GNOD vote: "This is the next opportunity we have to look at even more dense housing development." Advocates for expanding gateway zones, live-work spaces, micro-unit housing, and boardinghouse-style housing. Strong proponent of city-PHA partnership as the most reliable mechanism for actually delivering units.

**Nuance:** Frames housing as a workforce and equity issue — police, firefighters, teachers can't afford to live here. Data-driven, pragmatic tone. Not ideological about density but focused on what actually produces units.

entries.title: Rich Blalock
entries.body:
**Role:** City Councilor, currently serving multiple terms. Reelected 2025.

**Background:** Deep Portsmouth family roots — grandfather Dick Blalock served on City Council in 1970; father Jack Blalock served as Assistant Mayor and two terms as Mayor. Owner of Old Ferry Landing Restaurant. Assistant coach at Portsmouth High School. Served on Recreation Board, Historic District Commission, Land Use Committee, and multiple blue ribbon committees.

**Housing record:** Supported GNOD and ADU ordinance changes. Served on the Land Use Committee alongside Moreau and Kelley — the working group that drove much of the pre-GNOD zoning reform groundwork. Voted to ratify the Housing Blue Ribbon Committee structure (unanimous 8-0). Positions himself as pro-affordability but also pro-neighborhood character and historic preservation.

**Nuance:** Less ideologically housing-forward than Tabor, Moreau, or Cook. Comes at it from a small-business owner and community stewardship angle. The family legacy in Portsmouth politics gives him credibility with longtime residents who are skeptical of rapid change.

entries.title: Karen Conard
entries.body:
**Role:** City Manager of Portsmouth since January 2020. Chief Administrative Officer overseeing 800+ employees and a $131M municipal budget.

**Background:** BA in Political Science and French from Duke University. 30+ years in public sector. Career spans MA State Senate, Office of Governor William Weld, MBTA, MA Department of Housing and Community Development, MassDevelopment, and 11 years as Community Development and Planning Director in Methuen and Peabody MA. Immediately prior: Executive Director of Merrimack Valley Planning Commission. Former president of Housing Support Inc. (nonprofit serving low-income/homeless residents).

**Role in housing:** Executes council housing policy. Publicly explained the GNOD bonus incentive structure at the October 2024 council vote. All major projects — GNOD, Ruth Griffin Place, Lafayette Road — run through her office for implementation. The Housing Action Plan due July 2026 is a City Manager deliverable.

**Notes:** Unusual depth of housing-specific background for a city manager — MA DHCD, affordable housing nonprofit board, regional planning. Not just an administrator; she understands the financing and regulatory mechanics. Key relationship for PHA board members to cultivate.

entries.title: Gerry Duffy
entries.body:
**Role:** Founder and primary driver of Progress Portsmouth, Portsmouth's main pro-housing advocacy organization. Not elected, not a city insider — positioned explicitly as an outside watchdog. Self-described mission: "be the teeth."

**Background:** Working on Portsmouth housing advocacy for approximately 2 years. First member of the Portsmouth Housing Committee (PHC). Shares frustrations with the Portsmouth Listens process that many insiders also express. Uses multiple AI tools for research and analysis.

**Key contribution:** Built and maintains the 137-item Housing Action Plan framework — a comprehensive, versioned, public document tracking every housing action item the city should pursue. Council voted 9-0 on Feb 17, 2026 to use this framework as the basis for the official Housing Action Plan.

**Working relationship:** Maintains close relationships with Tabor, Moreau, and other housing-aligned council members. Works with Byron Matteo (GIS analysis) and Brian Chelner (Planning Board) as technical allies.

**Relevance to PHC:** Key outside ally. PHC members should coordinate with Gerry while maintaining independent judgment — his framework is the foundation, but PHC's triage and priorities should reflect board members' own analysis and political read.

**Contact:** progressportsmouth.com | github.com/gduffy44-svg/progress-portsmouth-tools

entries.title: Brian Chelner
entries.body:
**Role:** Chair of the Portsmouth Planning Board.

**Housing stance:** Pro-housing, supportive of innovative housing types. Notably a proponent of co-living as a model for increasing density affordably.

**Relevance:** Planning Board approves or rejects site plans and major zoning applications. As chair, Chelner sets the tone for how the board approaches housing proposals. PHC recommendations can be pushed to the Planning Board — Chelner is the key relationship on that body.

**Notes:** Planning Board has improved significantly in the last year per Progress Portsmouth and Gerry Duffy. Chelner was cited as a source for the HAP framework item on planning department capacity and workflow assessment.

entries.title: Byron Matteo
entries.body:
**Role:** Housing-related city employee. Also serves on Portsmouth School Board.

**Key contribution:** Conducted comprehensive GIS parcel analysis of NH State data (January 2026) that underpins much of the quantitative case for zoning reform in the HAP framework.

**Key findings from his GIS analysis:**
- 85.8% of Portsmouth residential land (2,276 acres) zoned exclusively single-family
- 14.2% (375 acres) permits multifamily
- 69.9% of single-family zone parcels don't meet current lot size requirements
- Multifamily zones generate $5.9M/acre in assessed value vs. $1.0M/acre for single-family — a 5.71x differential
- Zone-specific reform proposals: R zone to 1-acre minimums, SRA to 15,000 sf, SRB to 7,500 sf
- Single-family zones require 1.15x more linear infrastructure per parcel than multifamily zones

**Notes:** Technical ally for evidence-based housing reform. His GIS work is cited extensively throughout the HAP 137-item framework as the analytical foundation for single-family zone reform proposals.

entries.title: Rebecca Perkins Kwoka
entries.body:
**Role:** NH State Senator, Portsmouth district. Senate Democratic Leader.

**Housing relevance:** Portsmouth housing ally at the state level. Tabor has been working to engage her on Portsmouth housing priorities. Has sponsored legislation to restore state aid to higher education. Active on housing as a statewide issue.

**Notes:** Key state-level relationship for Portsmouth housing advocates. State legislative action is required for several HAP items including pillow tax authorization, inclusionary zoning enabling legislation, and MFTE. Perkins Kwoka is the natural champion for those efforts at the state house.
